title: Exodus 36
subtitle: Work Begins; Too Much Generosity; Curtains and Frames Constructed

sections.header: Verses 1—19 — The Skilled Workers Begin; The Tabernacle Curtains

heb.paragraph: V.5-7: "The people are bringing more than enough for doing the work the LORD commanded." Moses had to restrain the people from giving — they had to be told to stop. This is the only instance in the OT (or the NT) of an offering needing to be halted due to excess. The surplus was itself a spiritual indicator: Israel had moved from the golden calf (stealing from God's service for idolatry) to the Tabernacle (giving beyond the need). The direction of generosity reveals the direction of the heart.

V.1-2: Bezalel (bĕ-tsel-ʾēl — "in the shadow/protection of God") and Oholiab (ʾĂhōlîʾāb — "the Father is my tent") together supervised all the construction. Their names are theological: the craftsman lives "in God's shadow" and "under the Father's tent." The work of sacred artistry is done under divine protection and patronage. The integration of meaning in a craftsman's very name is a form of vocational consecration.

hist.historical: The construction account closely parallels the instructions in chapters 25–31, following the ancient literary convention of command-fulfilment narration found in Mesopotamian building inscriptions. Gudea's temple-building inscriptions from Lagash (c. 2100 BC) follow the same pattern: divine instruction, gathering of materials, skilled labour, construction. The cherubim woven into the tabernacle curtains used the tapestry-weaving technique attested in Egyptian and Mesopotamian textile production — surviving examples from Tutankhamun's tomb show similar skilled polychrome weaving.
hist.context: The restraint of the giving (vv.4–7) is the only moment in Scripture where an offering drive was stopped because the people gave too much. The craftsmen had to tell Moses: we cannot use what is being brought. Moses had to issue a public order to stop giving. This is the overflow of a repentant, Spirit-moved community — the golden calf's guilt resolved in extravagant generosity.

refs.note: David's Temple offering: "The people rejoiced at the willing response of their leaders, for they had given freely and wholeheartedly to the Lord." The same overflow of joy.
The Macedonian churches: "they gave as much as they were able, and even beyond their ability." The Tabernacle offering is the OT precedent for this NT phenomenon.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes this is the only restraint-of-giving command in the OT. The community's overflowing generosity is an act of covenant renewal — the same people who donated gold for the calf (32:2–3) now give more than the tabernacle can use. The excess giving is penitential as well as generous.

Alter notes the embarrassment of overabundant giving is uniquely human. The craftsmen come to Moses not with a problem but with the best possible news: there is too much. The restraint-of-giving command is the book's most unexpected moment of institutional modesty.

Calvin: 'It is rare for men to be so liberal that they must be restrained from giving. But this people, stirred by zeal of repentance, poured forth their offerings with such abundance that Moses had to stop them.' The excessive giving is covenant renewal in material form.

The NET note on 'the people were restrained from bringing more' explains Moses formally commanded cessation of offerings. The only parallel — Nehemiah's rebuilding project (Neh 7:70–72) — also records abundant voluntary giving, suggesting covenant renewal moments generate exceptional generosity.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter notes the embarrassment of overabundant giving is uniquely human. The craftsmen come to Moses not with a problem but with the best possible news: there is too much. The restraint-of-giving command is the book's most unexpected moment of institutional modesty.

Calvin: 'It is rare for men to be so liberal that they must be restrained from giving. But this people, stirred by zeal of repentance, poured forth their offerings with such abundance that Moses had to stop them.' The excessive giving is covenant renewal in material form.

The NET note on 'the people were restrained from bringing more' explains Moses formally commanded cessation of offerings. The only parallel — Nehemiah's rebuilding project (Neh 7:70–72) — also records abundant voluntary giving, suggesting covenant renewal moments generate exceptional generosity.

Calvin: 'It is rare for men to be so liberal that they must be restrained from giving. But this people, stirred by zeal of repentance, poured forth their offerings with such abundance that Moses had to stop them.' The excessive giving is covenant renewal in material form.

The NET note on 'the people were restrained from bringing more' explains Moses formally commanded cessation of offerings. The only parallel — Nehemiah's rebuilding project (Neh 7:70–72) — also records abundant voluntary giving, suggesting covenant renewal moments generate exceptional generosity.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'the people were restrained from bringing more' explains Moses formally commanded cessation of offerings. The only parallel — Nehemiah's rebuilding project (Neh 7:70–72) — also records abundant voluntary giving, suggesting covenant renewal moments generate exceptional generosity.

The NET note on the community being 'restrained from bringing more' discusses Moses' halting of the offerings as an extraordinary event in biblical history — the only case of a divinely commanded stop to voluntary religious giving. The note identifies the theological message: the people's generosity so exceeded what was needed that the excess would have become surplus, not service.

MacArthur: The people were bringing more than enough for the work. Moses gave an order to stop the offering. The only divinely-commanded halt to religious giving in Scripture. The superabundance of the offering is the measure of the covenant community's gratitude.

MacArthur: The verbal echo of God's instructions in the construction report establishes the criterion of success: did the workers do exactly what God commanded? Faithful correspondence to divine specification is the tabernacle's standard of success.

sections.header: Verses 20—38 — The Frames, Bars, Veil, and Entrance Curtain

heb.paragraph: V.8-13: The ten inner curtains were joined in two sets of five with loops and gold clasps — a unified whole assembled from coordinated parts. The number ten (ʿeser) in Tabernacle construction is systematic: ten curtains, ten sockets (for the altar), ten cubits of height. The architectural precision of the Tabernacle design reflects the theological precision of the covenant it housed: God's dwelling is not approximate but exact.

V.24-26: The silver sockets (ʾădānîm) into which the wall frames fitted were made from the census ransom money (30:11-16) — each Israelite's half-shekel formed the literal foundation of the Tabernacle. The house of God rested on the ransom of the people. Every Israelite, rich or poor, contributed equally; the foundation embodied the equal worth of every redeemed life before God.

hist.historical: The frame construction — forty-eight acacia-wood frames set in ninety-six silver sockets — represents substantial engineering. Each frame was approximately 4.5m tall and 0.7m wide. The silver for the sockets came from the census tax (38:25–28), making every Israelite male a literal foundation-contributor to the sanctuary. The crossbar system (five bars per side, the middle bar running the full length) provides structural rigidity consistent with portable military architecture documented in Egyptian campaign records.
hist.context: The detailed repetition of the Tabernacle construction (mirroring Exod 26) is intentional and theologically significant. The text could simply say "they did as commanded." Instead it repeats every measurement and material. The repetition is the message: Israel did exactly what God said, nothing more and nothing less. After the golden calf — where they did exactly what God did not say — the precise obedience of execution is its own theological statement.

refs.note: The execution follows the instructions of chapter 26 exactly. The repetition is not redundancy but liturgical affirmation: what God commanded, the people performed without deviation.

Solomon's temple veil and doors replace the tabernacle's curtain partitions. The function is identical — separating degrees of holiness — but the materials shift from portable fabric to permanent stone and cedar.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The construction of the inner curtains from linen with cherubim begins the process of building the most sacred interior of the tabernacle. Sarna notes the craftsmen begin with what is most interior — the heart of the sanctuary — and work outward.

The construction sequence — from the most interior outward — mirrors the approach sequence of covenant worship: one begins at the most sacred and works toward the public. Sarna notes this architectural logic expresses the covenant's theological priorities: the divine presence is the centre; everything else is ordered in relation to it.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The construction chapters maintain the same grammatical precision as the instruction chapters. Alter notes every verb of construction echoes a verb of instruction — 'he made' answering 'you shall make.' The correspondence is the narrative's covenant verification.

The construction chapters' exact verbal correspondence with the instruction chapters is the narrative's sustained form of theological point-making. Alter notes this technique — the correspondence verifiable by any reader who compares the chapters — establishes compliance as literally exact, not approximately faithful.

Calvin: 'The workmen began at the most holy part of the tabernacle, that it might be apparent their chief care was to provide God with a worthy habitation.' The interior priority reflects theological priority — the presence-space before the approach-space.

Calvin: The craftsmen's obedience to the divine pattern in every detail shows that worship acceptable to God must be grounded in divine instruction rather than human invention. The tabernacle's beauty is not decorative creativity but covenant fidelity — every detail matters because God specified every detail.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the ten linen curtains explains their dimensions (28 × 4 cubits each) and the joining method (loops and clasps). The note discusses how the craftsmen's execution of the exact dimensions specified in ch.26 is itself the covenant testimony of complete obedience.

The NET note on the goat hair curtains, ram-skin covering, and outer hide covering discusses the layered construction: the most precious inner layer (fine linen with cherubim) progresses to the most functional outer layer (hides). The note traces how this mirrors the graduated holiness of the sacred spaces within.

MacArthur: The people were bringing more than enough for the work. Moses gave an order to stop the offering. The only divinely-commanded halt to religious giving in Scripture. The superabundance of the offering is the measure of the covenant community's gratitude.

MacArthur: The verbal echo of God's instructions in the construction report establishes the criterion of success: did the workers do exactly what God commanded? Faithful correspondence to divine specification is the tabernacle's standard of success.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: The repetition is the message: Israel did exactly what God said, nothing more and nothing less…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"The people bring much more than enough for doing the work that the Lord has commanded us to do." So Moses gave command, and word was proclaimed throughout the camp, "Let no man or woman do anything more for the contribution for the sanctuary." So the people were restrained from bringing.NIV"The people are bringing more than enough for doing the work the Lord commanded to be done." Then Moses gave an order and they sent this word throughout the camp: "No man or woman is to make anything else as an offering for the sanctuary." And so the people were restrained from bringing more.

src.title: Narrative Repetition
src.quote: The repetition of the Tabernacle instructions in Exodus 25–31 (command) and 35–40 (execution) is a deliberate literary structure — sometimes called the "command-execution" pattern. Found in Egyptian building texts and Mesopotamian temple accounts, it confirms that the work was done exactly as ordered.
src.note: The literary repetition is not poor editing but deliberate theological emphasis: exact obedience.

src.title: Silver bases from census
src.quote: The forty silver bases under the north and south frames (vv.24,26) were cast from the census ransom silver (38:25–27) — every Israelite's half-shekel literally supporting the Tabernacle structure.
src.note: The Tabernacle rested on the ransom price of the whole community.

rec.text: The Macedonian overflow of giving → Paul uses it as the model for the Jerusalem collection. The Tabernacle giving is the OT paradigm for NT community generosity.

The command-execution pattern in Exod 35–40 became a model for understanding the relationship between divine revelation and human response throughout the OT: what God commands, his people execute faithfully.

rows.label: More Than Enough: The Offering Stopped
rows.text: Moses gave an order throughout the camp: no man or woman is to make anything else as an offering for the sanctuary. For what they already had was more than enough. The only biblical record of a divinely-commanded halt to religious giving.

rows.label: Verbal Echo: Construction as Faithful Correspondence
rows.text: The craftsmen built the tabernacle with ten curtains of finely twisted linen and blue, purple and scarlet yarn -- exactly as the LORD had instructed. The construction chapter's verbal echo of the instruction chapter demonstrates complete covenant correspondence.

tx.title: Tabernacle construction execution — abbreviated in LXX
tx.content: MT's construction account repeats the instructions from chapters 25–31 almost verbatim. The LXX significantly abbreviates this repetition, retaining the key actions but cutting redundant specification language. The SP follows MT's full repetition.
tx.note: The LXX's abbreviation is the most economical reading — avoiding word-for-word repetition of what was already stated. Whether the LXX translators shortened MT or had a shorter Vorlage is debated.

debate.title: Why Repeat the Tabernacle Instructions in Full?

positions.name: Covenant verification
positions.argument: The execution chapters confirm every instruction was followed. The reader witnesses total obedience — which is the theological point after the golden calf's total disobedience. The length is the message.

positions.name: Two independent sources combined
positions.argument: The instruction (P source) and execution accounts were originally independent documents combined by a later editor. The repetition reflects source combination rather than intentional literary structure.

coaching.tip: 'The people are bringing more than enough' (v. 5). Moses orders them to stop giving. Overflow generosity—unimaginable in fundraising logic. When hearts stir, scarcity inverts to surplus.

The detailed execution (vv. 8-38) mirrors the earlier blueprint. Repetition isn't redundancy—it's testimony. The craftsmen didn't improvise; they followed the pattern. Faithfulness looks like precision.
